Step 3¶

Student A – Macroeconomic & Geopolitical Data¶

In [3]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import yfinance as yf
from fredapi import Fred


# Setup: FRED API key (get one free from https://fred.stlouisfed.org/)

fred = Fred(api_key="54ae7c475447eb4a80c1fa586e19c513")


# Student A - Macroeconomic & Geopolitical Specialist

def get_macroeconomic_data(start="2015-01-01", end="2025-01-01"):
    """
    Collect 5 macroeconomic/geopolitical variables that affect oil prices.
    Returns a dataframe with aligned dates.
    """
    data = {}

    # 1. U.S. Inflation (CPI)
    data["US_CPI"] = fred.get_series("CPIAUCSL", observation_start=start, observation_end=end)

    # 2. U.S. GDP
    data["US_GDP"] = fred.get_series("GDP", observation_start=start, observation_end=end)

    # 3. U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) via Yahoo Finance
    dxy = yf.download("DX-Y.NYB", start=start, end=end, interval="1mo")["Close"].squeeze()
    data["USD_Index"] = dxy

    # 4. U.S. Treasury 10-Year Yield
    data["US10Y_Yield"] = fred.get_series("DGS10", observation_start=start, observation_end=end)

    # 5. WTI Crude Oil Price (proxy for supply-demand shocks)
    oil_price = yf.download("CL=F", start=start, end=end, interval="1mo")["Close"].squeeze()
    data["Crude_Oil_WTI"] = oil_price

    # Build DataFrame and align dates
    df_macro = pd.DataFrame(data)

    # Resample to monthly frequency
    df_macro = df_macro.resample("M").mean()
    return df_macro



# Visualization Function

def plot_macroeconomic_data(df_macro):
    sns.set(style="whitegrid", palette="muted", font_scale=1.1)

    # Plot 1: Time Series of All Variables
    df_macro.plot(subplots=True, figsize=(12, 12), linewidth=2, title="Macroeconomic & Geopolitical Variables")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    # Plot 2: Correlation Heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    sns.heatmap(df_macro.corr(), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", fmt=".2f")
    plt.title("Correlation Between Macroeconomic Variables")
    plt.show()

    # Plot 3: Pairplot
    sns.pairplot(df_macro.dropna())
    plt.suptitle("Pairwise Relationships of Macroeconomic Variables", y=1.02)
    plt.show()

    # Plot 4: Boxplots (outlier detection)
    df_long = df_macro.melt(var_name="Variable", value_name="Value")
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    sns.boxplot(x="Variable", y="Value", data=df_long)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.title("Distribution & Outliers of Macroeconomic Variables")
    plt.show()



# Run for Student A

df_macro = get_macroeconomic_data()
plot_macroeconomic_data(df_macro)

YF.download() has changed argument auto_adjust default to True

Student B – Microeconomic Specialist¶

In [5]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import yfinance as yf


# Student B - Microeconomic Specialist

def get_microeconomic_data(start="2015-01-01", end="2025-01-01"):
    """
    Collect 5 microeconomic company/industry-level variables that affect oil prices.
    Returns a dataframe with aligned dates.
    """
    tickers = {
        "ExxonMobil_XOM": "XOM",
        "Chevron_CVX": "CVX",
        "Halliburton_HAL": "HAL",
        "DeltaAir_DAL": "DAL",
        "Tesla_TSLA": "TSLA"
    }

    data = {}
    for name, ticker in tickers.items():
        df = yf.download(ticker, start=start, end=end, interval="1mo")["Close"].squeeze()
        data[name] = df

    df_micro = pd.DataFrame(data)
    df_micro = df_micro.resample("M").mean()  # monthly frequency
    return df_micro



# Visualization Function

def plot_microeconomic_data(df_micro):
    sns.set(style="whitegrid", palette="Set2", font_scale=1.1)

    # Plot 1: Line plot of all companies
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    for col in df_micro.columns:
        plt.plot(df_micro.index, df_micro[col], label=col, linewidth=2)
    plt.title("Microeconomic Company-Level Variables (Close Prices)")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Stock Price (USD)")
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()

    # Plot 2: Normalized Price Trend (all start at 100)
    normalized = df_micro / df_micro.iloc[0] * 100
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    for col in normalized.columns:
        plt.plot(normalized.index, normalized[col], label=col, linewidth=2)
    plt.title("Normalized Growth of Microeconomic Variables (Base = 100)")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Index (Base 100)")
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()

    # Plot 3: Rolling 12-month correlation with Exxon (XOM)
    rolling_corr = df_micro.rolling(12).corr(df_micro["ExxonMobil_XOM"])
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    for col in df_micro.columns:
        if col != "ExxonMobil_XOM":
            plt.plot(rolling_corr.index, rolling_corr[col], label=f"XOM vs {col}")
    plt.title("Rolling 12-Month Correlation with ExxonMobil")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Correlation")
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()

    # Plot 4: Heatmap of returns correlation
    returns = df_micro.pct_change().dropna()
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    sns.heatmap(returns.corr(), annot=True, cmap="viridis", fmt=".2f")
    plt.title("Correlation of Monthly Returns (Microeconomic Variables)")
    plt.show()



# Run for Student B

df_micro = get_microeconomic_data()
print("Columns in DataFrame:", df_micro.columns.tolist())
print("\nFirst 5 rows of data:")
print(df_micro.head())

plot_microeconomic_data(df_micro)

Student C – Financial Data & Plots¶

In [6]:
# Student C - Financial Data


import yfinance as yf
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

def get_financial_data(start="2000-01-01", end="2025-01-01"):
    tickers_C = {
        "Crude_Oil_ETF": "USO",
        "Brent_Oil_ETF": "BNO",
        "Energy_Sector_ETF": "XLE",
        "US_Treasury_Bonds": "TLT",
        "SP500_Index": "SPY"
    }

    data = {}
    for name, ticker in tickers_C.items():
        df = yf.download(ticker, start=start, end=end, interval="1mo")["Close"].squeeze()
        data[name] = df

    df_fin = pd.DataFrame(data)
    df_fin = df_fin.resample("M").mean()
    return df_fin



# Visualization Function

def plot_financial_data(df_fin):
    sns.set(style="whitegrid", font_scale=1.1)

    # Plot 1: Oil ETFs (USO vs BNO)
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    plt.plot(df_fin.index, df_fin["Crude_Oil_ETF"], label="USO (WTI Oil ETF)", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(df_fin.index, df_fin["Brent_Oil_ETF"], label="BNO (Brent Oil ETF)", linewidth=2)
    plt.title("WTI vs Brent Oil ETFs")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Price (USD)")
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()

    # Plot 2: Comparative performance (normalized to 100)
    normalized = df_fin / df_fin.iloc[0] * 100
    normalized.plot(figsize=(12, 6), linewidth=2)
    plt.title("Normalized Performance of Financial Variables (Base = 100)")
    plt.ylabel("Index (Base 100)")
    plt.show()

    # Plot 3: Rolling 12-month volatility of each ETF
    rolling_vol = df_fin.pct_change().rolling(12).std() * (12**0.5)
    rolling_vol.plot(figsize=(12, 6), linewidth=2)
    plt.title("Rolling 12-Month Volatility of Financial Variables")
    plt.ylabel("Volatility (Std Dev of Returns)")
    plt.show()

    # Plot 4: Heatmap of returns correlation
    returns = df_fin.pct_change().dropna()
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    sns.heatmap(returns.corr(), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", fmt=".2f")
    plt.title("Correlation of Monthly Returns (Financial Variables)")
    plt.show()



# Run for Student C

df_fin = get_financial_data()
print("Columns in DataFrame:", df_fin.columns.tolist())
print("\nFirst 5 rows of data:")
print(df_fin.head())

plot_financial_data(df_fin)

Step 5¶

Code: Student A – Outlier Cleaning (IQR method)¶

In [7]:
# Student A - Extreme Outlier Cleaning


import pandas as pd

def clean_outliers_iqr(df, factor=1.5, method="remove"):
    """
    Clean extreme outliers using the IQR method.
    
    Parameters:
        df (pd.DataFrame): Input dataset
        factor (float): IQR factor (default 1.5)
        method (str): "winsorize" (clip to bounds) or "remove" (drop outliers)
    
    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: Cleaned dataset
    """
    df_clean = df.copy()
    
    for col in df_clean.select_dtypes(include="number").columns:
        Q1 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.25)
        Q3 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.75)
        IQR = Q3 - Q1
        lower = Q1 - factor * IQR
        upper = Q3 + factor * IQR

        if method == "winsorize":
            # Cap extreme values
            df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].clip(lower, upper)
        elif method == "remove":
            # Drop rows with outliers in this column
            df_clean = df_clean[(df_clean[col] >= lower) & (df_clean[col] <= upper)]
    
    return df_clean



# Apply to All Datasets


# Example dictionary of datasets (replace with your actual variables)
datasets = {
    "Macro": df_macro,
    "Micro": df_micro,
    "Financial": df_fin
}

# Clean datasets
cleaned_datasets_A = {}
for name, df in datasets.items():
    cleaned = clean_outliers_iqr(df, method="remove", factor=1.5)
    cleaned_datasets_A[name] = cleaned
    print(f"\n{name} Data - Before: {df.shape}, After Student A Cleaning: {cleaned.shape}")
    print(cleaned.head())

Student B - Bad Data Cleaning¶

In [8]:
# Student B - Bad Data Cleaning


import pandas as pd

def clean_bad_data(df):
    """
    Clean bad data by handling duplicates, negative or zero values, and NaNs.
    
    Parameters:
        df (pd.DataFrame): Input dataset
    
    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: Cleaned dataset
    """
    df_clean = df.copy()

    # 1. Remove duplicate rows
    df_clean = df_clean[~df_clean.index.duplicated(keep="first")]

    # 2. Replace negative values with NaN where not realistic (e.g., prices or volumes)
    for col in df_clean.select_dtypes(include="number").columns:
        df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].apply(lambda x: x if x is not None and x >= 0 else None)

    # 3. Drop rows where all numeric values are missing or zero
    df_clean = df_clean.dropna(how="all", subset=df_clean.select_dtypes(include="number").columns)

    # 4. Drop any remaining NaN values
    df_clean = df_clean.dropna()

    return df_clean


# ============================
# Apply to All Datasets
# ============================

# Make sure df_macro, df_micro, df_fin are already defined before this
datasets = {
    "Macro": df_macro,
    "Micro": df_micro,
    "Financial": df_fin
}

# Clean datasets with Student B’s method
cleaned_datasets_B = {}
for name, df in datasets.items():
    cleaned = clean_bad_data(df)
    cleaned_datasets_B[name] = cleaned
    print(f"\n{name} Data - Before: {df.shape}, After Student B Cleaning: {cleaned.shape}")
    print(cleaned.head())  # Show first few rows

Student C - Missing Values Cleaning¶

In [9]:
# Student C - Missing Values Cleaning 


import pandas as pd

def clean_missing_values(df, method="interpolate"):
    """
    Handle missing values using filling or interpolation.
    
    Parameters:
        df (pd.DataFrame): Input dataset
        method (str): "ffill", "bfill", "interpolate"
    
    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: Cleaned dataset
    """
    df_clean = df.copy()

    if method == "ffill":
        df_clean = df_clean.ffill()  # forward fill
    elif method == "bfill":
        df_clean = df_clean.bfill()  # backward fill
    elif method == "interpolate":
        # First try forward fill and backward fill, then interpolate remaining gaps
        df_clean = df_clean.ffill().bfill()
        df_clean = df_clean.interpolate(method="linear")
    else:
        raise ValueError("Method must be one of: 'ffill', 'bfill', 'interpolate'")

    return df_clean



# Apply to All Datasets


datasets = {
    "Macro": df_macro,
    "Micro": df_micro,
    "Financial": df_fin
}

cleaned_datasets_C = {}
for name, df in datasets.items():
    cleaned = clean_missing_values(df, method="interpolate")
    cleaned_datasets_C[name] = cleaned
    print(f"\n{name} Data - Before: {df.shape}, After Student C Cleaning: {cleaned.shape}")
    print(cleaned.head())

2000-03-31          37.645351    95.007324  
2000-04-30          37.645351    91.903564  
2000-05-31          37.645351    90.458664

Step 6¶

Sterilized Dataset (Group Cleaning)¶

In [10]:
# Step 6 - Sterilized Dataset (Group Cleaning)


import pandas as pd

# Student A Cleaning
def clean_outliers_iqr(df, factor=1.5, method="remove"):
    df_clean = df.copy()
    for col in df_clean.select_dtypes(include="number").columns:
        Q1 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.25)
        Q3 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.75)
        IQR = Q3 - Q1
        lower = Q1 - factor * IQR
        upper = Q3 + factor * IQR
        if method == "winsorize":
            df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].clip(lower, upper)
        else:  # remove
            df_clean = df_clean[(df_clean[col] >= lower) & (df_clean[col] <= upper)]
    return df_clean

# Student B Cleaning 
def clean_bad_data(df):
    df_clean = df.copy()
    df_clean = df_clean.drop_duplicates()
    df_clean = df_clean.replace([0, -999, -99], pd.NA)  # replace common bad codes
    return df_clean

# Student C Cleaning
def clean_missing_values(df, method="interpolate"):
    df_clean = df.copy()
    if method == "ffill":
        df_clean = df_clean.ffill()
    elif method == "bfill":
        df_clean = df_clean.bfill()
    else:  # interpolate
        df_clean = df_clean.ffill().bfill().interpolate(method="linear")
    return df_clean


# Apply All Cleaning


# Combine all datasets into one big DataFrame
df_all = pd.concat([df_macro, df_micro, df_fin], axis=1)

# Step 1: Remove outliers (Student A)
df_all = clean_outliers_iqr(df_all, method="remove", factor=1.5)

# Step 2: Remove bad/questionable data (Student B)
df_all = clean_bad_data(df_all)

# Step 3: Handle missing values (Student C)
df_all = clean_missing_values(df_all, method="interpolate")

print("\nFinal Sterilized Dataset Shape:", df_all.shape)
print(df_all.head())

Final Sterilized Dataset Shape: (12, 15)

Step 7¶

Student A – Distributional Plots¶

In [11]:
# Student A - Distributional Plots


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

def plot_distributional(df, dataset_name):
    """
    Create distributional plots: histograms, KDE, boxplots.
    """
    numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include="number").columns

    for col in numeric_cols:
        plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))

        # Histogram + KDE
        plt.subplot(1,2,1)
        sns.histplot(df[col].dropna(), kde=True, bins=30, color="skyblue")
        plt.title(f"{dataset_name} - {col} Distribution")

        # Boxplot
        plt.subplot(1,2,2)
        sns.boxplot(x=df[col].dropna(), color="lightgreen")
        plt.title(f"{dataset_name} - {col} Boxplot")

        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()


# Apply to all datasets
for name, df in cleaned_datasets_A.items():   # <- use your cleaned datasets
    plot_distributional(df, name)

Student B – Time Series Plots¶

In [12]:
# Student B - Time Series Plots


def plot_time_series(df, dataset_name):
    """
    Plot time series of all numeric variables.
    """
    plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
    for col in df.select_dtypes(include="number").columns:
        plt.plot(df.index, df[col], label=col)

    plt.title(f"{dataset_name} - Time Series Overview")
    plt.xlabel("Time")
    plt.ylabel("Value")
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.show()


# Apply to all datasets
for name, df in cleaned_datasets_A.items():
    plot_time_series(df, name)

Student C – Multivariate Plots¶

In [13]:
# Student C - Multivariate Plots

def plot_multivariate(df, dataset_name):
    """
    Create multivariate plots: scatter matrix and correlation heatmap.
    """
    numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include="number").dropna()

    if numeric_df.shape[1] > 1:
        # Correlation heatmap
        plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
        sns.heatmap(numeric_df.corr(), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", center=0)
        plt.title(f"{dataset_name} - Correlation Heatmap")
        plt.show()

        # Pairplot (scatter plots + histograms)
        sns.pairplot(numeric_df, diag_kind="kde", corner=True)
        plt.suptitle(f"{dataset_name} - Pairplot", y=1.02)
        plt.show()
    else:
        print(f"{dataset_name} has only one variable, skipping multivariate plots.")


# Apply to all datasets
for name, df in cleaned_datasets_A.items():
    plot_multivariate(df, name)
